  • Five types of mafu'ul will be covered later in the Tasheel course.

docs.title: Lessons and Practical Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Oppressor and oppressed cannot coexist: Peace is impossible while one side actively oppresses the other. The oppressor must stop for genuine coexistence.

  2. Islam permits coexistence: Muslims can and historically did live alongside non-Muslims. The condition is that non-Muslims must not prevent Muslims from practicing their religion.

  3. Trust in Allah's timeline: Believers must not despair if change takes a long time. Allah is timeless. Every sacrifice contributes to the eventual outcome.

  4. Worship alone is insufficient: Salah, fasting, and other acts of ibadah are only part of the deen. True closeness to Allah (iqtirab) requires implementing Islam comprehensively -- in personal, social, economic, and political life.

  5. Do not follow the oppressor's system: The command "la tuti'hu" means Muslims should not adopt the values, systems, and lifestyles of those who oppress and disobey Allah. Islam provides complete guidance for all aspects of life.

  6. The Ummah's roadmap is in Ayah 19: (a) Do not obey the oppressor, (b) worship Allah, (c) draw closer to Allah through comprehensive implementation of Islam. The help of Allah is conditional on following this roadmap.

  7. Darul Islam: It is fard (obligatory) on Muslims in Muslim-majority countries to establish a Darul Islam -- a state that welcomes all Muslims, grants them citizenship based on faith, and implements Islamic law fully. Muslims in non-Muslim countries should raise awareness but are not individually obligated to establish this.

  8. Living with restrictions: Muslims in non-Muslim countries have covenants/treaties with their states. They should follow local laws, practice as much of their deen as possible, and make dua for the establishment of Darul Islam.

  9. Sunnah vs. Fard priorities: Following the sunnah is important, but it should not come at the expense of neglecting fard obligations (e.g., focusing on outward sunnah appearances while engaging in interest-based transactions).

docs.title: Historical Context and Examples
docs.text:
• Abu Jahl: Referenced as the direct context of the surah -- he attempted to prevent the Prophet (SAW) from praying, embodying the taghi/zalim described in the ayat.
  • Fir'aun: Remembered not for the pyramids or great works, but for his tyranny against Musa (AS). This is part of Allah's plan -- oppressors are remembered for their oppression.
  • Namrud, Haman, Shaddad, Abu Lahab: All historical oppressors who tried to stop the prophets and their followers. All were destroyed by Allah.
  • Jewish tribes in Medina: Banu Qurayza, Banu Qaynaqa, Banu Nazeer -- lived alongside Muslims under treaty. Punished only when they betrayed their agreements.
  • Prophet (SAW) in Mecca: Lived 13 years among a majority mushrik population, demonstrating that Islam allows coexistence in the absence of active oppression.
  • Contemporary context: The Palestinian situation was discussed as an example of zalim-mazlum dynamics and why true coexistence cannot exist under oppression.

docs.title: Q&A Highlights
docs.text:
• Q: Is everything non-Muslims do (selling alcohol, usury) considered oppression? A: Not necessarily. If Muslims have the option not to participate, it is not the type of oppression (zulm) described in this surah. The oppression in question is actively preventing Muslims from practicing their deen.

  • Q: What does iqtirab (drawing closer) look like practically? A: Implementing Islam in every stage of life -- personal, private, public, social. Not just ibadah. There are restrictions for Muslims in non-Muslim countries, but establishing Darul Islam is fard on Muslim-majority nations.

  • Q: How do we verify if wazayif (prescribed litanies) are legitimate? A: If the wazifa is in Arabic, check if it is a Quranic ayah, a hadith, or a dua ma'thur (transmitted supplication). If it can be traced to Quran and Sunnah, it is fine. If it is in a foreign/unknown script, it is suspect and potentially actual magic.

  • Q: Allah says He has no emotions, but the Quran mentions Allah's anger and the hadith mention Allah smiling. A: This is an aqidah (theology) topic that requires dedicated time. To be addressed in a future session.

End of Surah Al-Alaq. Next session: Surah Al-Qadr.

docs.title: TFS 101 - Study Notes
docs.text: Date: December 7, 2023 Surah Covered: Surah Al-Qadr (Chapter 97)

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covers the complete tafseer of Surah Al-Qadr (5 ayaat), including its connection to the preceding Surah Al-Alaq, the significance of Laylatul Qadr, the role of angels, and a Q&A segment addressing free will, divine attributes, and related topics.

docs.title: Connection to the Previous Surah (Surah Al-Alaq)
docs.text:
• Surah Al-Alaq discussed ilm (knowledge) and ta'leem (teaching): "allama bil-qalam" and "allama al-insana ma lam ya'lam."
  • Surah Al-Qadr continues that theme by explaining how Allah teaches humanity -- through the revelation of the Quran on Laylatul Qadr.
  • First connection: "Allama al-insana ma lam ya'lam" (He taught man what he did not know) is fulfilled through the Quran's revelation in Laylatul Qadr.
  • Second connection: The subject of Surah Al-Alaq is the human being; the subject of Surah Al-Qadr is the Quran. This sequence shows that the Quran is the essential tool for a human to achieve their purpose in life.
  • The purpose of life (maqsad) -- success in dunya and akhira (sa'ada fi darain, bliss of both worlds) -- can only be achieved through the Quran.

docs.title: Ayah 1: "Inna anzalnaahu fi Laylatul Qadr"
docs.text:
"Indeed, We revealed it in the Night of Power."

  • Inna -- harf mushabbah bil-fi'l (particle resembling a verb), used for emphasis ("indeed").
  • Na -- damir muttasil (attached pronoun) for first person plural ("We"). Originally "inna na" but one noon is dropped.
  • Anzalnaahu -- from the root anzala/yunzilu/inzalan, meaning "to reveal." The pronoun "hu" (it) refers to the Quran.
  • Fi Laylatul Qadr -- mudaf/mudaf ilayh construction meaning "in the Night of Power."

Key Tafseer Point -- The Quran is Not Reactionary: - Human literature is typically produced as a reaction to events (e.g., constitutions after revolutions, analyses after conflicts). - Allah emphasizes that the Quran was NOT revealed as a reaction to any event. It was part of Allah's pre-eternal plan (decided in al-Azal). - Because it was planned, not reactive, the best speech (the Quran) was paired with the best time (Laylatul Qadr). - This pairing reflects protocol -- just as heads of state receive certain protocols, the Quran (which is the "head of all") deserves the highest protocol of being revealed at the most honored time.

docs.title: Ayah 2: "Wa ma adraaka ma Laylatul Qadr"
docs.text:
"And what can make you know what the Night of Power is?"

  • Adra/yudri -- to make someone aware.
  • Ma -- interrogative particle ("what"), used rhetorically to build anticipation and emphasize the greatness of Laylatul Qadr.

docs.title: Ayah 3: "Laylatul Qadri khayrun min alfi shahr"
docs.text:
"The Night of Power is better than a thousand months."

  • A thousand months is approximately 84 years.
  • Context from hadith: A Sahabi expressed concern that previous nations had longer lifespans (100-120 years) dedicated to worship, while this ummah's average lifespan is 60-70 years (as stated by the Prophet). How could they ever compete?
  • The Prophet recited this surah as the answer: one night of worship in Laylatul Qadr equals 1,000 months of worship.
  • Calculation example: If a person worships on Laylatul Qadr for 45 years (age 15 to 60), that equals 45 x 84 = 3,780 years of worship.

docs.title: Ayah 4: "Tanazzalul malaa'ikatu war-Roohu feeha bi idhni Rabbihim min kulli amr"
docs.text:
"The angels and the Spirit descend therein, by the permission of their Lord, for every affair."

  • Al-Malaa'ika -- the angels.
  • Ar-Rooh -- Jibreel (alayhi as-salam).
  • Bi idhni Rabbihim -- by the permission of their Lord.
  • Min kulli amr -- for every affair/matter.

Key Tafseer Points: - Jibreel alone is powerful enough to protect the revelation, yet Allah sent additional angels as protocol for the Quran. - The Prophet saw only Jibreel during the first revelation, but other angels were also present (just as angels are always around us unseen). - The revelation occurring in Ramadan provides an extra layer of security since the shayateen are locked up, preventing any claim that they could have tampered with the Quran.

Connection to Surah Al-Dukhan: - The "Laylatul Mubarakah" (blessed night) mentioned in Surah Al-Dukhan is the same as Laylatul Qadr -- NOT the 15th of Sha'ban (Laylatul Bara'ah). - There is no mention of the 15th of Sha'ban anywhere in the Quran. - In Surah Al-Dukhan, Allah says every wise affair is distributed on this night -- referring to Laylatul Qadr.

The Two Groups of Angels (Al-Mala'ul A'la and Al-Mala'ul Safil): - Al-Mala'ul A'la (the Superior Group): - Includes the highest-ranking angels. - Also includes the souls (arwah) of the prophets after they pass away. - Sometimes includes the souls of very pious individuals. - Their role: constantly making du'a for righteous people and du'a against evil people. - Referenced in the Quran: "Join the group of My servants" (addressing the soul at death). - Al-Mala'ul Safil (the Lower Group): - The angels of the lower heavens and earth.

How Divine Decrees Are Distributed: 1. Allah discloses major decisions to Al-Mala'ul A'la first. 2. Upon receiving the command, they all prostrate in sajdah. 3. The information cascades down from the seventh heaven to the first heaven. 4. Specific tasks are assigned to designated angels (e.g., Malak al-Mawt is informed about who will die that year; angels assigned to unborn children are told the child's lifespan, rizq, and whether they will be fortunate or miserable in deeds). 5. All of this assignment happens on Laylatul Qadr -- not on the 15th of Sha'ban.

docs.title: Ayah 5: "Salaamun hiya hatta matla'il fajr"
docs.text:
"Peace it is, until the emergence of dawn."

  • The entire night is blessed -- every single moment and second from beginning until Fajr.
  • It is not the case that only parts of the night are blessed; the blessing is continuous throughout.

docs.title: Two Revelations of the Quran
docs.text:
1. First revelation: The entire Quran was revealed from al-Lawh al-Mahfuz to Bayt al-Izzah (in the lowest heaven) -- this happened on Laylatul Qadr.
  2. Second revelation: The Quran was revealed piecemeal from Bayt al-Izzah to the heart of the Prophet over 23 years -- the first five ayaat of Surah Al-Alaq were also revealed on a Laylatul Qadr.

docs.title: When is Laylatul Qadr?
docs.text:
• It occurs in the month of Ramadan.
  • The Prophet advised the ummah to seek it in the last ten nights, especially the odd nights (21st, 23rd, 25th, 27th, 29th).
  • It is NOT fixed on the 27th every year:
  • Ubayy ibn Ka'b swore it was on the 27th, but he was referring to that specific year, not establishing a permanent rule.
  • Those who worship only on the 27th and neglect other odd nights risk missing Laylatul Qadr entirely.
  • Common mistake: Many masajid complete the Quran on the 27th, and people treat the 28th and 29th as if nothing remains to be done -- this is incorrect.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Inna Indeed (particle of emphasis) Harf mushabbah bil-fi'l Particle resembling a verb Damir muttasil Attached pronoun Anzala / yunzilu / inzalan To reveal / reveals / revelation Laylatul Qadr The Night of Power/Decree Mudaf / Mudaf ilayh Possessive construction (annexed/annexed to) Adra / yudri To make someone aware Al-Mala'ul A'la The Superior Assembly (highest group of angels + prophets' souls) Al-Mala'ul Safil The Lower Assembly (lower-ranking angels) Arwah Souls (plural of rooh) Rooh Spirit; here refers to Jibreel (AS) Malak al-Mawt The Angel of Death Azal Pre-eternity (before creation) Maqsad Purpose/objective Sa'ada fi darain Bliss/happiness in both worlds (dunya and akhira) Bulugh Puberty / age of religious responsibility Ibadat Acts of worship Qudrah Power/ability (divine omnipotence) Sifat Attributes/characteristics (of Allah) Rida Pleasure/approval (of Allah) Majboor Forced/compelled Wahey / Wahi Divine revelation Laylatul Mubarakah The Blessed Night (synonym for Laylatul Qadr, from Surah Al-Dukhan) Laylatul Bara'ah The 15th night of Sha'ban Yufraqu kullu amrin hakeem Every wise affair is distributed (from Surah Al-Dukhan)

docs.title: The Purpose (Maqsad) of Surah Al-Qadr
docs.text:
• To direct our attention to our attitude toward the Quran.
  • To demonstrate that the Quran is not a reactionary text but a divinely planned revelation deserving the highest honor.
  • To highlight the immense blessing of Laylatul Qadr as an equalizer for this ummah's shorter lifespans.

docs.title: 1. Does the decree about a baby's fortune apply before the soul is breathed in?
docs.text: • The decree (fortunate or miserable in deeds) is based on Allah's knowledge of what the person will do. It is given to the angel to record, but it reflects Allah's foreknowledge, not predetermination that removes free will.

docs.title: 2. Does Allah have emotions?
docs.text:
• Allah does not have emotions -- emotions are weaknesses that come and go.
  • Allah has sifat (attributes/characteristics) which are permanent.
  • When the Quran says Allah is "angry" or "pleased," it means:
  • Angry = He punishes the person.
  • Pleased = He rewards the person.
  • The effect is on the person, not on Allah Himself.
  • Regarding hadith about Allah "smiling" or "laughing":
  • Approach 1: Allah does smile/laugh, but not like any of His creation. We affirm it without knowing the "how" (based on "laysa kamithlihi shay" -- nothing is like Him).
  • Approach 2 (ta'weel): These expressions are interpreted as meaning Allah is pleased (rida) with that person.

docs.title: 3. Does "Allah guides whom He wills" negate free will?
docs.text:
• This ayah is bayanul qudrah -- an explanation of Allah's power/ability, not a statement that He forcefully guides or misguides.
  • Allah has the power to make everyone a believer, but He did not will that -- humans are given free will.
  • Allah guides those who are willing to be guided (referenced from Surah Al-Layl): those who give, fear God, and affirm the truth -- Allah makes the path of ease (Jannah) easy for them.
  • Ultimate guidance comes from Allah, but it is in response to a person's willingness.

docs.title: 4. Can missed prayers be made up through multiplied reward on Laylatul Qadr or at the Haram?
docs.text:
• No. The multiplication of reward (e.g., Laylatul Qadr = 1,000 months, prayer in Haram = 100,000 times reward) applies to extra/nafl reward, not to making up missed obligatory prayers (qada).
  • A person must still make up every missed prayer individually.

docs.title: 5. Does the soul that dies early in the womb go to heaven?
docs.text:
• If life had begun (the soul was breathed in), then yes, that baby will be in Jannah waiting for the parents.
  • If formation was not complete and the soul had not been breathed in, then it is not counted as a human life in that sense.

docs.title: 6. Free will vs. predestination
docs.text:
• Humans have free will; they are not majboor (forced).
  • Allah already knows what each person will do, but this foreknowledge does not negate free will.
  • This is a deep topic that requires extended discussion.

docs.title: Lessons and Practical Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Seek Laylatul Qadr throughout the last ten odd nights of Ramadan -- do not limit worship to the 27th night alone.
  2. The Quran is essential for human purpose -- just as Surah Al-Alaq discusses the human, Surah Al-Qadr immediately follows with the Quran, showing the two are inseparable.
  3. Laylatul Qadr is an equalizer -- despite shorter lifespans, this ummah can accumulate thousands of years of reward through consistent worship on this night.
  4. Worship the entire night -- from the beginning of the night until Fajr, every moment is blessed. Do not assume only parts of the night count.
  5. The Quran demands reverence -- its revelation was given the highest protocol (best time, angelic escort), which should shape our attitude toward it.
  6. Multiplied rewards do not replace missed obligations -- there is no shortcut for qada prayers through multiplication of reward.
  7. Allah's attributes are not human emotions -- understanding the difference between sifat (permanent divine attributes) and human emotions is important for correct aqeedah.
  8. Guidance is a two-way relationship -- Allah guides those who take the initiative to seek guidance through good deeds, affirming truth, and taqwa.

docs.title: TFS 101 - Study Notes
docs.text: Date: December 14, 2023 Surah Covered: Surah Al-Bayyinah (Chapter 98) - Ayat 1-3 (with introduction to Ayah 4)

docs.title: Session Title
docs.text: Surah Al-Bayyinah (The Clear Evidence) - Part 1: Why the Disbelievers Denied the Quran

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Al-Bayyinah The Clear Evidence Munfakkeen To be abandoned / to be left alone Suhuf (singular: Sahifah) Pages / Scriptures Mutahharah Purified Kutub Qayyimah Correct / upright writings Ahlul Kitab People of the Book (Jews and Christians) Mushrikeen Polytheists / those who commit shirk Wahi Divine revelation Mu'jizat Miracles (of the prophets) Anbiya Prophets (plural of Nabi) Risalah Prophethood / message Min Bayaniyyah The particle "min" used for explanation/specification (i.e., "namely") Perennialism Religious pluralism; the view that all religions share a single metaphysical truth Kabira (sins) Major sins Adhab al-Qabr Punishment of the grave Sarab Mirage

docs.title: Arabic Grammar Points
docs.text:
• Lam Yakun: Originally yakunu, but because of the particle "lam" (negation of past), it becomes yakun (jazm). It requires an ism (subject) and khabar (predicate).
  • Ism of Kana: "Alladheena kafaru min Ahlil Kitab wal-Mushrikeen" is the ism.
  • Khabar of Kana: "Munfakkeen" is the khabar, which must be in the nasb case (hence the ya-nun ending). If it were in the raf' case, it would be "munfakkoon."
  • Alladheena: Relative pronoun meaning "those who."
  • Min Bayaniyyah: When "min" comes after a relative pronoun (alladheena, allati, alladhi), it functions as explanatory (bayaniyyah), meaning "namely" or "i.e." -- not partitive ("some of").

docs.title: Connection Between Surahs (Munasabah)
docs.text:
The instructor highlighted the thematic flow across three consecutive surahs:

  1. Surah Al-Alaq: Discusses the creation of the human being and the sources of knowledge for success in dunya and akhirah. Revelation is identified as one important source of knowledge.
  2. Surah Al-Qadr: Discusses the significance and importance of wahi (revelation), its ranking among the sources of knowledge.
  3. Surah Al-Bayyinah: Explains the reason why some people deny the wahi -- the focus of this session.

docs.title: Structure of Surah Al-Bayyinah
docs.text:
The surah contains 8 ayat and is divided into three segments:

Segment Ayat Content 1 1-3 Consolation to Rasulullah (SAW): some Ahlul Kitab and Mushrikeen will not believe; do not be concerned 2 4-5 The Ahlul Kitab forming alliance with the Mushrikeen; their historical pattern of denying prophets and miracles 3 6-8 Condemnation of such disbelievers, calling them the worst of all creations

docs.title: Makki or Madani?
docs.text:
• Madani position: Because Ahlul Kitab are mentioned, and there were no Ahlul Kitab in Makkah, so it must have been revealed in Madinah.
  • Makki position (stronger): Although Ahlul Kitab were not physically in Makkah, they became aware of the Prophet's (SAW) growing influence and began coaching the Mushrikeen with objections to raise against him. The alliance had already started forming before the Hijrah, even if it was not at its peak. Therefore, mentioning Ahlul Kitab does not automatically make the surah Madani.
  • Conclusion: Most likely Makki.

docs.title: Theme of the Surah
docs.text: The central theme is explaining the real reason why the Ahlul Kitab and Mushrikeen denied the Quran: - Their denial was not based on genuine doubts. - It was rooted in jealousy (hasad) and arrogance (kibr). - The Ahlul Kitab were expecting the final prophet to come from among them (Bani Isra'il), and were shocked that he was an Arab from Bani Isma'il. - The Mushrikeen did not want to abandon their Jahili (pre-Islamic) practices. - Neither group could produce a valid counter-argument against the Quran, yet they still rejected it.

docs.title: Ayah 1-3: The Clear Evidence
docs.text:
• Allah states that the disbelievers from the Ahlul Kitab and the Mushrikeen were not going to be left alone (abandoned) until the clear evidence came to them.
  • The clear evidence (Al-Bayyinah) is Rasulullah (SAW) himself -- a messenger from Allah reciting purified pages containing correct writings.
  • The disbelievers demanded evidence on their own terms, but Allah declares that the evidence will be defined by Him, not by their demands.

docs.title: The Absurd Demands of the Disbelievers
docs.text:
Examples of what they demanded: 1. Fire from heaven: They demanded that Allah accept the Prophet's sacrifice by sending fire from the sky to consume it, as a sign. 2. Individual purified pages: They demanded that purified pages with clear guidance be sent down directly to each individual person, untouched by any angel or human.

Why these demands were absurd: - If every person received direct revelation, everyone would become a prophet. - If everyone is a prophet, there is no ummah (community of followers), no test, and no trial. - Angels already worship Allah without disobedience -- there would be no purpose in creating humans if everyone were upon direct guidance with no free will in choosing.

docs.title: The Wisdom of Sending Prophets as Human Role Models
docs.text:
• Shaykh Abdul Qadir al-Jilani (rahimahullah) stated: "A man can be successful only when he sees successful people."
  • Prophets were sent as human role models so that people could see someone from their own community practicing the divine commands, proving it is achievable.
  • Without a visible example, people would not be able to follow divine commands.
  • This principle applies in worldly life as well: success and motivation come from observing others who have succeeded.

docs.title: What is Perennialism?
docs.text:
• A philosophy that views all religious traditions as sharing a single metaphysical truth.
  • In practical terms: all Ahlul Kitab (Jews, Christians, Muslims) are on the right path and will attain salvation without needing to accept Islam.
  • The instructor warned that this trend is growing among some modernist and non-traditional scholars.

docs.title: The Perennialist Argument from This Ayah
docs.text: • They cite the word "min" in "alladheena kafaru min Ahlil Kitab" to argue that only some of the Ahlul Kitab are disbelievers, while others are believers who will enter Paradise.

docs.title: The Refutation (from the Ahl al-Sunnah wal-Jama'ah position)
docs.text:
1. Contextual argument: The full phrase is "min Ahlil Kitab wal-Mushrikeen." If "min" means "some of," then it would also mean only some Mushrikeen are disbelievers -- which no one claims. All Mushrikeen are disbelievers by consensus. The same logic must apply to Ahlul Kitab in this verse.
  2. Grammatical argument: When "min" follows a relative pronoun (alladheena), it is called "min bayaniyyah" -- it serves to explain and specify, not to partition. It means "namely" or "i.e." So: "Those who disbelieved, namely the Ahlul Kitab and the Mushrikeen."
  3. Quranic evidence: Allah says: "Qul ya ayyuhannas inni Rasulullahi ilaykum jami'an" (Say: O people, I am the Messenger of Allah to you all). The Prophet's (SAW) message is universal -- for all of mankind. Anyone who rejects it after receiving it is a disbeliever, regardless of their personal character.
  4. Good deeds of disbelievers: Allah addresses this in Surah al-Nur with the parable of the mirage (sarab) -- disbelievers will think their good deeds have value, but when they approach them on the Day of Judgment, they will find nothing, like a traveler in a desert who sees water from a distance but finds nothing upon arrival.
  5. Courtroom analogy: A murderer cannot be acquitted simply because he is a kind person in other aspects of life. Similarly, disbelief is a fundamental offense that is not nullified by good personal character. A disbeliever with good character may receive a lighter punishment, but the punishment remains eternal.

docs.title: Lessons and Practical Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Jealousy and arrogance are the root causes of denying truth. When people reject clear evidence, it is often not due to intellectual doubt but due to ego and envy. This applies in matters of faith and daily life.

  2. The same objections recur across time. The objections raised by atheists and skeptics today are fundamentally the same as those raised during the Prophet's (SAW) time. Understanding the Quran's responses equips believers to address modern skepticism.

  3. Role models are essential for guidance. The wisdom behind sending human prophets is that people need visible, relatable examples to follow. In personal development and community life, being a good example is one of the most effective forms of da'wah.

  4. Beware of perennialism and religious pluralism. The idea that all religions lead to salvation contradicts the Quranic position that belief in the final Prophet (SAW) is necessary for salvation after his coming. Coexistence and respect for others does not require compromising core theological beliefs.

  5. Context is critical in understanding the Quran. Isolating a single word (like "min") from its context can lead to fundamentally incorrect conclusions. Always consider what comes before and after.

  6. Good personal character does not substitute for correct belief. While good character is valued, it does not compensate for the rejection of the fundamental message of Tawhid and Prophethood.

  7. Allah defines the evidence, not the creation. The disbelievers wanted evidence on their own terms, but Allah provides guidance according to His wisdom. Submission (Islam) means accepting Allah's terms, not dictating one's own.

docs.title: Q&A Highlights
docs.text:
Q: Does the ayah in Surah Al-Ma'idah where Isa (AS) says "If You punish them, they are Your servants; and if You forgive them, You are the Almighty, the Wise" mean Christians have a special chance of forgiveness?

A: No. This ayah (in tu'adhibhum fa'innahum ibaduk, wa in taghfir lahum fa'innaka antal Azizul Hakeem) refers to the followers of Isa (AS) who committed shirk. Isa (AS) is speaking to Allah on the Day of Judgment about those who went astray after him. Before Rasulullah (SAW), the followers of Isa (AS) were required to believe in Isa (AS). After the coming of Rasulullah (SAW), belief in him and his message became obligatory for all of mankind.

docs.title: References Mentioned
docs.text:

  • Surah Al-Qadr (Chapter 97)
  • Surah Al-Bayyinah (Chapter 98), Ayat 1-4
  • Surah Al-Ma'idah (5:118) -- Isa (AS) speaking to Allah about his followers
  • Surah Al-A'raf (7:158) -- "Qul ya ayyuhannas inni Rasulullahi ilaykum jami'an"
  • Surah Al-Nur (24:39) -- Parable of the mirage (sarab) for the deeds of disbelievers
  • Shaykh Abdul Qadir al-Jilani (rahimahullah) -- Quote on seeing successful people
  • Debate with an atheist (referenced by the instructor, conducted in Urdu)

docs.title: TFS 102 - Tafseer of Surah Al-Bayyinah (The Clear Evidence)
docs.text: Date: January 18, 2024 Surah: Al-Bayyinah (Surah 98)

docs.title: Key Themes and Topics
docs.text:
• The demands of the disbelievers (Ahl al-Kitab and Mushrikeen) for a "clear evidence" before accepting Islam
  • Allah's refutation of their demands by pointing to their own historical record of rejecting evidence
  • The two categories of humanity: Sharr al-Bariyyah (worst of creatures) and Khayr al-Bariyyah (best of creatures)
  • Arrogance as the root of disbelief vs. Khashyah (fear/humility) as the root of faith
  • Modern parallels: atheist and agnostic objections mirror the same ancient demands
  • Discussion of perennialism (referenced from previous class)
  • Q&A covering: grammar points, Aqeedah on Kalam Allah, Shia-Sunni relations, geopolitics (Houthis/Iran), the authority of the Jama'ah

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Al-Bayyinah The clear evidence / clear proof Ahl al-Kitab People of the Book (Jews and Christians) Mushrikeen Polytheists Munfakkeen To be abandoned / separated Suhuf Pages / scriptures Mutahharah Purified Kutub Qayyimah Correct / upright writings Din al-Qayyimah The correct / upright religion Mukhliseen Being sincere / with sincerity Hunafa' Being upright / inclining toward truth Sharr al-Bariyyah Worst of all creatures Khayr al-Bariyyah Best of all creatures Khashyah Fear of Allah / reverential awe / humility Kibr Arrogance / pride Jannatu Adn Gardens of eternity / permanent gardens Alwah (sing. Lawh) Tablets (as given to Musa) Fi'l Naqis Incomplete verb (grammatical term for kana) Ism Mawsul Relative pronoun (e.g., alladhina) Silah The clause that completes the relative pronoun Dhameer al-Fasl Pronoun of separation (used for exclusivity/emphasis) Taqiyah Dissimulation (concealing one's true beliefs) Tahrif al-Quran Distortion / corruption of the Quran

docs.title: Main Tafseer Points - Ayah by Ayah
Ayah 1: The Demand for Clear Evidence
docs.text:
"Lam yakun alladhina kafaru min Ahl al-Kitab wal-Mushrikeen munfakkeena hatta ta'tiyahum al-Bayyinah"

  • Both the People of the Book (Jews) and the polytheists set a condition: they would only believe if a clear evidence (Bayyinah) came to them.
  • Their demanded "clear evidence" was: a messenger from Allah (not Muhammad, but an angel) who comes personally to them, reciting purified pages containing correct writings.
  • The Mushrikeen learned these argumentative tactics from the Jews, because the Mushrikeen had no academic tradition of their own. The Mushrikeen excelled only in poetry about women, horses, wine, and battlefield bravery -- not theological debate.
  • The Mushrikeen would travel to consult with the Jews in Madinah and elsewhere about how to respond to the Prophet's claims.

docs.title: Ayah 2: The Nature of the Demanded Evidence
docs.text:
"Rasulun min Allahi yatlu suhufan mutahharah, fiha kutubun qayyimah"

  • This defines what the disbelievers meant by "Bayyinah": a messenger from Allah reciting purified pages containing correct writings.
  • Grammatical note: "suhufan mutahharah" is mawsuf-sifah (noun-adjective); "suhufan" is the maf'ul (object) of "yatlu."

docs.title: Ayah 3: Allah's Refutation -- Their Own Track Record
docs.text:
"Wa ma tafarraqa alladhina utu al-Kitab illa min ba'di ma ja'at-hum al-Bayyinah"

  • Allah responds: You (People of the Book) were already given clear evidence before through Musa (alayhi al-salam) in the form of the Alwah (tablets) with clear divine commandments.
  • What happened? You became divided. Some followed the tablets; others started worshipping a cow (as told in Surah Taha).
  • The argument: If you rejected clear evidence in the past, what guarantee is there that you would accept it now? On what moral ground do you demand more evidence?

docs.title: Ayah 4: What They Were Actually Commanded
docs.text:
"Wa ma umiru illa li-ya'budu Allaha mukhliseena lahu al-deen, hunafa', wa yuqimu al-salah, wa yu'tu al-zakah, wa dhalika din al-qayyimah"

  • The commands in the evidence given to them were straightforward:
  • Worship Allah with utmost sincerity (ikhlas)
  • Be upright (hunafa')
  • Establish prayer (salah)
  • Give zakat
  • Instead of following these commands:
  • Some Jews began worshipping a cow
  • Some Jews called Uzair the son of Allah ("Wa qalat al-Yahud Uzairun ibn Allah")
  • Some Christians worshipped Isa ibn Maryam
  • They abandoned salah and zakat
  • Key rhetorical connection: They demanded "kutub qayyimah" (correct writings), but Allah had already given them "din al-qayyimah" (the correct religion). The book is only a part of the religion. They wasted the entire system, so demanding just a part of it again is baseless.
  • When Isa (alayhi al-salam) came to revive and correct their religion, they declared him a false messiah and tried to kill him -- further evidence of their pattern of rejection.

docs.title: Ayah 5: Sharr al-Bariyyah -- The Worst of Creatures
docs.text:
"Inna alladhina kafaru min Ahl al-Kitab wal-Mushrikeen fi nar Jahannam khalideena fiha, ula'ika hum sharr al-bariyyah"

  • Those who disbelieve from the People of the Book and the polytheists will be in the Hellfire eternally.
  • They are declared "sharr al-bariyyah" -- the worst of all creatures.
  • Refutation of perennialism: This ayah explicitly states that disbelievers from Ahl al-Kitab will be in Hellfire forever, contradicting the view that People of the Book may eventually enter Jannah.
  • Logical argument raised: If someone claims only "some" of Ahl al-Kitab are disbelievers (not all), would they say the same about Mushrikeen? Both groups are mentioned identically in the same ayah. No one claims some Mushrikeen are believers, so the same logic must apply to Ahl al-Kitab who reject Islam.

docs.title: Ayah 6: Khayr al-Bariyyah -- The Best of Creatures
docs.text:
"Inna alladhina amanu wa 'amilu al-salihat, ula'ika hum khayr al-bariyyah"

  • Those who believe AND do righteous deeds are the best of all creatures.
  • Both conditions are required: Iman (belief) alone is not sufficient; it must be accompanied by 'amal salih (righteous deeds).
  • Grammatical point: "amanu" and "'amilu al-salihat" are both part of the silah (relative clause) of the ism mawsul "alladhina." The conjunction "wa" makes "'amilu" ma'tuf (conjoined) onto "amanu."
  • The pronoun "hum" in "ula'ika hum khayr al-bariyyah" is dhameer al-fasl: it restricts khayr al-bariyyah exclusively to this group. Without "hum," the sentence would merely describe them as being among the best; with "hum," it limits the category to them alone.

docs.title: Ayah 7-8: The Reward and Its Condition
docs.text:
"Jaza'uhum inda Rabbihim jannatu Adnin tajri min tahtiha al-anhar, khalideena fiha abada. Radiya Allahu anhum wa radu anh. Dhalika liman khashiya Rabbah."

  • The reward is with their Lord (not necessarily in this dunya).
  • The reward includes:
  • Gardens of eternity (Jannatu Adn)
  • Rivers flowing beneath them
  • Eternal abode (khalideena fiha abada)
  • Allah declares permanent pleasure with them ("Radiya Allahu anhum") -- He will never be angry with them again
  • They are also pleased with Allah ("wa radu anh")
  • The critical condition: "Dhalika liman khashiya Rabbah" -- This reward is for whoever fears/reveres his Lord.
  • Khashyah (reverential fear / humility) is the distinguishing quality of Khayr al-Bariyyah, just as kibr (arrogance) is the distinguishing quality of Sharr al-Bariyyah.

docs.title: Lessons and Practical Takeaways
docs.text:
1. The nature of belief (iman): Belief, by definition, pertains to the unseen. Demanding to see Allah or receive direct angelic revelation as a precondition for belief misunderstands what faith is. You do not "believe" in what you see -- you simply know it. Belief is for what you cannot see.

  2. Modern objections are not new: Atheists, agnostics, and skeptics today raise the same objections the Quraysh and Jews raised 1400+ years ago -- "show me God," "send me an angel," "program my brain to believe." The Quran has already addressed these arguments.

  3. False equivalence fallacy: When atheists cite Ibrahim (alayhi al-salam) asking Allah to show him resurrection and demand the same treatment, they commit a false equivalence. Ibrahim already believed in Allah and merely sought additional reassurance for his heart. A disbeliever who rejects Allah entirely is in no way comparable.

  4. Arrogance is the root of disbelief: The surah connects kufr with arrogance (kibr) -- the belief that one is too enlightened or intellectually advanced for divine guidance. This applies to modern atheists who dismiss religion as "dogma" while adhering to their own forms of dogmatism.

  5. Humility (khashyah) is the root of faith: Conversely, being included among the best of creatures requires khashyah -- humility, taqwa, and reverential fear of Allah. These are the opposite of arrogance.

  6. Iman requires action: Being among Khayr al-Bariyyah requires both belief and righteous deeds (wa 'amilu al-salihat). Faith without action is insufficient.

  7. Historical patterns of rejection: The People of the Book had a track record of receiving divine evidence and then rejecting or distorting it (worshipping the calf, calling Uzair the son of God, rejecting Isa). This pattern undermines their moral standing to demand further evidence.

  8. Din al-Qayyimah vs. Kutub Qayyimah: The correct writings they demanded were only a part of the correct religion they had already received and wasted. Demanding a subset of what you already rejected in its entirety is incoherent.

  9. The reward of the Hereafter is not guaranteed in this life: "Jaza'uhum inda Rabbihim" -- the reward is with their Lord. Believers should not expect full reward in the dunya.

  10. The greatest reward: Beyond the physical gardens and rivers of Jannah, the greatest reward is Allah's permanent pleasure ("Radiya Allahu anhum") -- that He will never again be angry with the people of Paradise.

docs.title: Historical Context and Related Stories
docs.text:
• The Golden Calf (referenced from Surah Taha): After Musa received the tablets from Allah, a faction of Bani Israel began worshipping a golden calf, despite having just received the clear divine evidence.
  • Uzair (Ezra) and the claim of divine sonship: Some Jews declared Uzair to be the son of Allah, paralleling how Christians declared Isa to be the son of Allah.
  • Isa (alayhi al-salam): Came to revive and correct the religion of Bani Israel. Instead of accepting him, they declared him a false messiah and attempted to kill him.
  • The Mushrikeen consulting the Jews: The Quraysh, lacking theological sophistication, would travel to consult with the Jews about how to argue against the Prophet Muhammad (sallallahu alayhi wa sallam).

docs.title: Arabic Grammar Points Discussed
docs.text:
• Fi'l Naqis (Incomplete Verb): "Kana" is a fi'l naqis; "yakunu" is its fi'l mudari' (present tense). With "lam," it becomes "lam yakun" (was not). The shortening (from yakunu to yakun) is related to sarf.
  • Ism Mawsul and Silah: "Alladhina" is the relative pronoun; the verb clause after it is the silah (relative clause). A silah can consist of two conjoined verbs (e.g., "amanu wa 'amilu al-salihat" together form one silah for one ism mawsul).
  • Mawsuf and Sifah: "Suhufan mutahharah" -- noun (suhuf) described by its adjective (mutahharah).
  • Dhameer al-Fasl: The pronoun "hum" in "ula'ika hum khayr al-bariyyah" serves as a separating/exclusive pronoun, restricting the quality of "best of creatures" exclusively to the described group.
  • Ma'tuf and Ma'tuf Alayh: In "amanu wa 'amilu al-salihat," "amanu" is the ma'tuf alayh (that which is conjoined upon) and "'amilu al-salihat" is the ma'tuf (the conjoined element).

docs.title: On Kalam Allah (Speech of Allah) and Aqeedah
docs.text:
• The Maturidi, Ashari, and Athari schools all agree that Allah's Kalam (speech) as a divine attribute is uncreated (ghayr makhluq).
  • The disagreement is on a secondary detail: when a human recites the Quran, is that human's recitation (the physical act and sound) created (makhluq)?
  • Imam Bukhari held the position that his own recitation of the Quran is makhluq (created), while the Kalam of Allah itself is not. He was expelled from Bukhara for this position and died in the village of Khartan on its outskirts.
  • This is a minor scholarly difference that should not lead to excommunication (takfir) of other Muslims.

docs.title: On Shia-Sunni Relations
docs.text:
• The majority of the Ummah historically does not make takfir of Shia laypeople (awam).
  • The takfir trend became popular in the 1980s, especially in the subcontinent and among some Saudi scholars.
  • Senior Saudi scholars like Bin Baz distinguished between Shia scholars and Shia laypeople.
  • If a Shia explicitly believes in Tahrif al-Quran (corruption of the Quran), then takfir applies. But the majority of Shias outwardly state they do not believe in this.
  • Muslims are responsible for judging based on the apparent (zahir), not suspicions of taqiyah.

docs.title: On Geopolitics (Houthis, Iran, Yemen)
docs.text:
• The instructor views the Houthi actions as geopolitics, not a war between Islam and Kufr.
  • Iran uses proxy/guerrilla warfare through various groups (Syria, Iraq, Yemen, Pakistan).
  • The Houthis are supported and enabled by Iran; without Iran's support, they would not be acting as they are.
  • A point was raised: the Houthis fire missiles toward distant Israel but do not target a nearby Israeli military base just across the sea from Yemen -- questioning the sincerity of their stated intentions.
  • Theoretical cooperation with ideologically different groups is permissible, but one should be skeptical of motives.

docs.title: On the Authority of the Jama'ah
docs.text:
• The hadith "Yad Allahi 'ala al-Jama'ah" (The Hand of Allah is over the group) means that the larger body of Muslims is upon the truth.
  • "Jumhur" (majority) is a recognized concept: if the majority of the Ummah believes or practices something, it is generally correct.
  • This Ummah is protected from collective misguidance, unlike previous nations. Ayat describing "most people" as misguided refer to all of mankind, not specifically to the Muslim Ummah.

docs.title: TFS 102 - Study Notes
docs.text: Date: January 25, 2024 Surah Covered: Surah Al-Zilzal (Al-Zalzalah) - Chapter 99

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covers the complete tafseer of Surah Al-Zilzal (The Earthquake), which describes the events of the Day of Judgment. The discussion includes the surah's theme, its connection to preceding surahs (Al-Alaq through Al-Bayyinah), detailed verse-by-verse explanation, and the concept of Alam al-Mithal.

docs.title: Key Themes and Topics
docs.text:
• The Day of Judgment (Yawm al-Qiyamah): The surah describes the destruction of the earth and the events that follow, including resurrection and accountability.
  • Metaphysical vs. Physical Reality: The Day of Judgment is a metaphysical reality that cannot be understood or judged through the laws of physics. Miracles, Jannah, Jahannam, Mi'raj, and Qiyamah all fall outside the scope of scientific inquiry.
  • Allah's Complete Knowledge: Allah knows every action, public and private, and has established a multi-layered testimony system to ensure perfect justice.
  • Accountability for Every Deed: No deed, no matter how small (even the weight of an atom), will be overlooked -- whether good or evil.
  • Refutation of Three Mushrikeen Misconceptions:
  • The universe is eternal and will never be destroyed.
  • Allah cannot possibly know all the actions of all people at all times.
  • Even if there is accountability, the idols will intercede and guarantee their success.

docs.title: Connection with Previous Surahs (Al-Alaq to Al-Zilzal)
docs.text:
The surahs from Al-Alaq onward form a connected sequence centered on the human being (insan):

  1. Surah Al-Alaq (96): Creation of the human being, Islamic epistemology, division of people into mu'min and kafir.
  2. Surah Al-Qadr (97): Deen cannot be understood by human reasoning alone; divine guidance (the Quran) was revealed on Laylatul Qadr.
  3. Surah Al-Bayyinah (98): Some people rejected the divine guidance and tried to stipulate conditions upon Allah; their recompense is Jahannam.
  4. Surah Al-Zilzal (99): The Day of Judgment when that recompense will actually be administered.

docs.title: Verse 1: Idha zulzilat al-ardu zilzalaha
docs.text:
"When the earth is shaken with its [final] earthquake"

  • This refers to the actual Day of Judgment, not merely a sign of it. Ordinary earthquakes are signs of the end times (akhir al-zaman), but this mega earthquake IS the Day of Judgment.
  • It will destroy the entire planet -- incomparably greater than any earthquake in human experience.

docs.title: Verse 2: Wa akhrajat al-ardu athqalaha
docs.text:
"And the earth brings forth its burdens"

  • First meaning: After destruction, Allah will resurrect the dead. The bodies buried in the earth are its "burdens" (athqal), and they will be brought forth for judgment. This includes all bodies -- buried, cremated, or drowned. Nothing leaves the planet earth.
  • Second meaning: The treasures hidden underground (gold, silver, diamonds, lithium, oil) will be brought forth. This is a sign preceding the Day of Judgment and is being witnessed today.

docs.title: Verse 3: Wa qala al-insanu ma laha
docs.text:
"And the human being says, 'What is wrong with it?'"

  • During the mega earthquake, as the earth collapses, people will panic and cry out in confusion. They will run to save their lives but find no safe place.

docs.title: Verse 4: Yawma'idhin tuhaddithu akhbaraha
docs.text:
"That day it will report its news"

  • The earth itself will testify for or against every person. Every piece of land where a good deed was performed will testify in favor of the person, and every piece of land where a sin was committed will testify against them.
  • This answers the mushrikeen's claim that Allah cannot know all human actions: not only does He know, He will have the very earth testify as a witness.

docs.title: Verse 5: Bi-anna rabbaka awha laha
docs.text:
"Because your Lord will have inspired [i.e., commanded] it"

  • The earth will give its testimony because Allah will inspire and command it to do so.

docs.title: Verse 6: Yawma'idhin yasduru al-nasu ashtatan li-yuraw a'malahum
docs.text:
"That day people will proceed in scattered groups to be shown their deeds"

  • People will not walk together in one mass. They will be grouped by their deeds:
  • Good groups: Anbiya (Prophets), Siddiqeen, Saliheen
  • Evil groups: Those who committed zina, those who spread corruption, kuffar, mushrikeen, munafiqeen, mulhideen, etc.

docs.title: Verses 7-8: Fa man ya'mal mithqala dharratin khayran yarahu / Wa man ya'mal mithqala dharratin sharran yarahu
docs.text:
"Whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it. And whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it."

  • Allah will not miss a single deed, no matter how small.
  • "Man" here is ism mawsool, used for intelligent beings (as opposed to "ma").
  • The word "yarahu" (will see it) is significant -- people will literally SEE their deeds, not just read about them.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Zilzal / Zalzalah Earthquake; the masdar (verbal noun) of zulzila Zulzilat Was shaken (fi'l majhool / passive voice) Idha When (adverb of time / zarf al-zaman); when added to fi'l madhi, it gives the meaning of fi'l mudareh (future) Athqal Burdens, heavy loads; plural -- refers to the dead buried in the earth or underground treasures Tuhaddithu Will report, will narrate Akhbar News, reports Awha Inspired, revealed, commanded Ashtatan Scattered groups Dharrah Atom (smallest possible weight) Mithqal Weight, equivalent to Maf'ul Mutlaq Absolute/cognate object -- a grammatical construction where the masdar is used as an object (explains why zilzalaha is in the nasb case) Ism Mawsool Relative pronoun; "man" is used for intelligent beings, "ma" for non-intelligent Alam al-Mithal The parallel realm of forms/manifestation where every action has a tangible copy Istihala Conversion/transformation -- a fiqh concept where a substance completely changes its nature, name, and taste Yawm al-Qiyamah The Day of Judgment (encompasses destruction) Yawm al-Hashr The Day of Resurrection/Gathering Barzakh The intermediate realm between death and resurrection Hikmah Wisdom -- a step beyond ilm (knowledge); knowing when and how to act on knowledge

docs.title: Multi-Layered Testimony System on the Day of Judgment
docs.text:
Allah has established multiple layers of witnesses so that no person can deny their deeds:

  1. Angels on the shoulders: They record every good and bad deed continuously.
  2. One's own body/limbs: A person's skin and limbs will testify against them. When the kuffar ask their limbs why they testified, they will reply: "Allah who gave everything the ability to speak gave us the ability to speak" (referenced from Surah Fussilat).
  3. The earth itself: Every piece of land will testify about the deeds performed upon it.

The purpose of this system is to establish complete justice -- so no one can make excuses or deny their actions.

docs.title: Alam al-Mithal (The Realm of Forms)
docs.text:
• A parallel realm where Allah has created tangible forms/copies of every single thing that happens in this universe.
  • Every action (amal) we perform has a form or shape stored in Alam al-Mithal.
  • On the Day of Judgment, these stored forms will be shown to people, and they will immediately recognize their own deeds with full clarity, unable to deny them.
  • Shah Waliullah al-Muhaddith al-Dahlawi (12th century Hijri scholar) proved this concept through 19 ahadith.
  • This concept explains how people will literally "see" their deeds (yarahu) as stated in verses 7-8.

docs.title: Metaphysics vs. Physics
docs.text:
• The Day of Judgment is a metaphysical reality (ma wara al-tabi'ah).
  • Scientific theories (Big Freeze, etc.) should not be used to validate or invalidate Quranic descriptions of Qiyamah.
  • For about 90 years, scientists have accepted the universe has a beginning and will eventually collapse -- but the Quranic narrative has stated this from day one.
  • Mu'jizat, Jannah, Jahannam, Mi'raj, and the events of Qiyamah cannot be understood through the laws of physics.

docs.title: Yawm al-Qiyamah Duration
docs.text:
• The Day of Judgment is 50,000 years long (referenced from Surah Al-Ma'arij).
  • It encompasses multiple stages: destruction, then resurrection (hashr), then accountability.

docs.title: Lessons and Practical Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Every deed matters: Even an atom's weight of good or evil will be seen. There is no such thing as a deed too small to count.
  2. No place is private from Allah: The very ground you stand on will testify about what you did. This should inspire mindfulness of our actions everywhere.
  3. Worldly success is not a sign of divine approval: The mushrikeen believed their worldly prosperity meant they were favored. This surah refutes that assumption entirely.
  4. Science cannot replace faith in the unseen: While science may align with some Quranic descriptions, metaphysical realities stand on their own authority and do not depend on scientific validation.
  5. No intercession without Allah's permission: The mushrikeen's belief that their idols would intercede for them is false. Success in the Hereafter depends on one's deeds, not false intercessors.
  6. Be conscious of the earth beneath you: Perform good deeds in many places so the earth becomes a witness in your favor, not against you.

docs.title: Halal Food and Cheese (Fiqh Discussion)
docs.text:
• Vegetarian food from non-Muslim restaurants (e.g., cheese pizza) is permissible if one is satisfied the ingredients are halal.
  • Cheese and Rennet: Rennet (used in cheese-making) can come from cows or pigs. The key fiqh concept is istihala (complete transformation) -- if a substance undergoes complete conversion where it loses its original nature, name, and taste, it may become permissible.
  • For cow-derived rennet, the animal must have been slaughtered properly (Islamically) for it to be halal without istihala.
  • Trusting a Muslim restaurant owner who claims halal is sufficient; one does not need to investigate packaging. Halal certification organizations add another layer of assurance.
  • Reference given: Sharia Board (New York), Mufti Idris -- they provide detailed presentations on halal standards.

docs.title: Difference Between Ilm and Hikmah
docs.text:
• Ilm (Knowledge): Broad -- knowing information and facts.
  • Hikmah (Wisdom): More specific -- knowing how, when, and whether to act upon knowledge. Not every piece of knowledge should be acted upon in every situation.
  • Example: Knowing someone committed a sin is ilm; approaching them with encouragement toward tawbah rather than condemnation is hikmah.
  • Hikmah is gained over time through maturity and increased knowledge; there is no shortcut.

docs.title: Engaging with Doubters and Deists
docs.text:
• A person who says "I believe in Allah but not in Islam, the Quran, or Salah" is classified as a kafir (specifically, a deist).
  • When engaging with such people, the advised approach is to ask questions rather than defend. Turn their objections back on them:
  • If someone rejects Islam for being "a political system," ask why they accept other political systems.
  • If someone says Islam is "not perfect," ask them to define perfection and then test their own standard against their own beliefs.
  • This method of engagement is rooted in Islamic dialectical theology (Ilm al-Kalam), which will be studied in the third year of the curriculum.

docs.title: Ilm al-Kalam
docs.text:
• Translated as "dialectical theology" (not merely "theology").
  • Involves categorization, disputation, and reasoning tools beyond just discussing the attributes (sifat) of Allah.

docs.title: Arabic Grammar Notes (from the session)
docs.text:
• Idha + fi'l madhi = future meaning: Idha reverses the tense of fi'l madhi to give a future (mudareh) meaning. This is the opposite of idh, which when used with fi'l mudareh gives a past (madhi) meaning.
  • Zilzalaha in nasb case: Because it functions as maf'ul mutlaq (cognate/absolute object). All five types of maf'ul take the nasb case.
  • Man vs. Ma (ism mawsool): "Man" is used for intelligent beings; "Ma" is used for non-intelligent entities.
  • Al-ardu as fa'il: In verse 2, al-ardu is the doer (subject) of the verb akhrajat.
  • Athqalaha -- idafah construction: Athqal is mudaf, ha (pronoun referring to al-ard) is mudaf ilayh.

docs.title: TFS 102 - Study Notes
docs.text: Date: February 1, 2024 Surah Covered: Surah Al-Aadiyat (Chapter 100)

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: A complete tafseer of Surah Al-Aadiyat, a short Makki surah. The surah uses the imagery of war horses to deliver a powerful message about human ungratefulness toward Allah and the consequences of being consumed by love of wealth.

docs.title: Key Themes and Topics
docs.text:
• Human ungratefulness (kufran al-ni'mah): The central theme -- Allah directs attention to how ungrateful human beings are despite all they have been given.
  • The loyalty and sacrifice of horses vs. the ingratitude of humans: Horses sacrifice their lives for owners who merely provide them food, yet humans fail to show gratitude to Allah who both created them and sustains them.
  • Love of wealth (hubb al-maal) as the root cause of ungratefulness: Intense attachment to dunya distracts people from remembering and thanking Allah.
  • Accountability on the Day of Judgment: A reminder that death is certain, secrets will be exposed, and everyone will be held accountable.
  • Connection to Surah Al-Zilzal (Ch. 99): The previous surah described the Day of Judgment; this surah provides the reasoning for why it is necessary -- to establish justice for human ingratitude.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Transliteration Meaning العاديات Al-Aadiyat The racers / racing horses (from adaa ya'du -- to run) ضبحا Dabhan Panting / casting their breath (sound horses make while racing) الموريات Al-Muriyaat The producers of sparks (from iraa' -- sparking) قدحا Qadhan Striking (producing sparks from hooves on stone) المغيرات Al-Mugheeraat The chargers / those that charge (from ighaara -- to charge/attack) صبحا Subhan At dawn / early morning نقعا Naq'an Dust جمعا Jam'an Collectively / together; also a gathering (of the enemy) كنود Kanood Extremely ungrateful شهيد Shaheed Witness بعثر Bu'thira Scattered / raised (from graves) حصل Hussila Made apparent / obtained / brought out خبير Khabeer All-Aware

docs.title: Arabic Grammar Points Discussed
docs.text:
• Wow of Qasam (oath): The "wa" in "wal-aadiyaati" is harf al-jarr used for taking an oath.
  • Fa of 'Atf (conjunction): The "fa" in "fal-muriyaati" is a conjunction particle. The rule of 'atf: the i'raab of the ma'tuf (what comes after) follows the i'raab of the ma'tuf 'alayhi (what comes before). That is why al-muriyaati carries kasra -- it follows the jarr from the wow of qasam.
  • Verb conjugation -- jam'a mu'annath gha'ib: "Atharna" is the third-person feminine plural past tense (athara > atharun > atharat > atharna).
  • Inna and its ism: In "innal-insaana," insaan is in the nasab (accusative) case because the ism of inna is always mansub.
  • Lam of emphasis (tawkeed): In "lakanood" and "lashaheed," the lam is added on the khabar of inna for additional emphasis. This rule is studied in Tasheel.
  • Word order for emphasis: "Lirabbihi lakanood" -- the expected order would be "lakanood lirabbihi," but Allah placed "lirabbihi" first to emphasize that it is specifically toward the Rabb (Lord) that he is ungrateful.

docs.title: Verses 1-5: The Oath of the War Horses
docs.text:
"By the racing horses, panting; producing sparks, striking; charging at dawn; raising dust; penetrating into the center collectively."

  • Allah takes an oath by war horses, describing five qualities:
  • They race at full speed (al-aadiyaat)
  • They pant heavily from exertion (dabhan)
  • Their hooves strike sparks from rocks (al-muriyaat qadhan)
  • They charge in battle at dawn (al-mugheeraat subhan)
  • They raise clouds of dust and plunge into the center of enemy lines (atharna naq'an, wasatna jam'an)
  • These horses face death willingly -- enemy soldiers would target the horse first to bring down the rider.
  • The horses never complain, never show laziness, and are always ready when their owner calls them to battle.

docs.title: Verse 6: "Indeed, mankind is ungrateful to his Lord"
docs.text:
• The connection between the horses and ungrateful humans: The horses are grateful to owners who merely provide them food (food that Allah created). If animals with lesser consciousness can show such loyalty and sacrifice, what excuse does the human being have for being ungrateful to Allah -- who not only provides for him but actually created him?
  • The question posed: Have ungrateful humans become worse than animals?
  • "Kanood" indicates an intense, deep-rooted ungratefulness.
  • This applies to both mushrikeen and mu'mineen alike -- ungratefulness is not exclusive to disbelievers.

docs.title: Verse 7: "And indeed, he is a witness to that"
docs.text:
• The human being himself is a witness to his own ungratefulness.
  • If you ask your own heart honestly, you know whether you are truly grateful or not.
  • Reference to a hadith: The Prophet (SAW) said to seek fatwa (ruling/verdict) from your own heart.
  • A related statement (not hadith): "One who recognizes himself recognizes Allah."
  • Self-recognition begins with acknowledging our own ignorance -- we do not even fully understand our own five senses (e.g., the science of vision: images land inverted on the retina but appear correctly in the mind, and science still cannot fully explain this).
  • Our ignorance about ourselves is proof (daleel) that there must be a Manager of all affairs -- Allah.

docs.title: Verse 8: "And indeed, his love of wealth is surely intense"
docs.text:
• This verse identifies the root cause of ungratefulness: intense love of dunya and wealth.
  • No matter how much wealth a person has, they always want more -- this is human nature, amplified by capitalism and lifestyle inflation.
  • Constant focus on increasing wealth causes a person to forget Allah.

docs.title: Verses 9-10: "Does he not know -- when what is in the graves is scattered..."
docs.text:
• A reminder that death is certain. Every person must choose: die with love of wealth, or die with love of Allah.
  • You will be raised on the Day of Judgment with whatever love dominated your heart.

docs.title: Verse 10: "...and what is in the hearts is obtained (made apparent)"
docs.text:
• All secrets will be exposed on the Day of Judgment.
  • Even if a person denies his ungratefulness in this world and puts up a front of piety, the truth of his heart will be revealed.

docs.title: Verse 11: "Indeed, their Lord about them, that day, is surely All-Aware"
docs.text: • Allah is All-Aware of everything at all times, not only on that Day. But because the context is about the Day of Judgment, He mentions His awareness on that Day specifically.

docs.title: Connection Between Surah Al-Zilzal (99) and Surah Al-Aadiyat (100)
docs.text:
• Surah Al-Zilzal described WHAT will happen on the Day of Judgment (earthquake, earth revealing its secrets, deeds displayed).
  • Surah Al-Aadiyat provides the REASONING for WHY the Day of Judgment is necessary: to establish justice. Allah created and blessed humanity, yet humanity is ungrateful -- this is injustice, and injustice demands accountability, which demands a Day of Judgment.

docs.title: Lessons and Practical Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Self-assessment of gratitude: Regularly ask yourself whether you are truly grateful to Allah. Your own heart is a witness -- be honest with it.
  2. Beware of love of wealth: The constant desire for more money and material comfort is identified as the primary barrier to gratitude. Monitor your attachment to dunya.
  3. Learn from the example of the horses: If animals can show complete loyalty and sacrifice to an owner who merely feeds them, we should show far greater devotion to the One who created us and everything we have.
  4. Acknowledge your ignorance: Recognizing how little we truly understand -- even about our own bodies -- is the starting point for recognizing Allah's greatness and our dependence on Him.
  5. Death is certain -- choose wisely: You will be raised with whatever love fills your heart. Make sure it is love of Allah, not love of dunya.
  6. Nothing is hidden from Allah: Even if you outwardly claim to be grateful, Allah knows the reality of your heart, and it will be exposed on the Day of Judgment.
  7. The Quran is interconnected: Every surah connects to the ones around it. Understanding these connections requires study, knowledge of Arabic, tafseer, and hadith -- not casual reading.

docs.title: Historical and Contextual Notes
docs.text:
• Direct vs. indirect addressees of the Quran: The Arabs of Makkah and Madinah were the direct addressees. They understood horses and camels intimately. We are secondary addressees who learn through scholarship and study.
  • Battle strategy -- attacks at dawn: The Sunnah of the Prophet (SAW) was to attack at dawn, not at night. The reasoning: at dawn, people are awake and visible, so fighters can distinguish combatants from women and children. The pre-Islamic Arab practice was to attack at night, but the Prophet changed this.
  • Universality of the Quran: A question was raised about how the Quran can be universal when it references things (like camels) that some cultures may not know. The answer: this is true of every field of knowledge, including science. Not everyone understands everything by reading alone -- knowledge is transmitted through those who understand it to those who do not. The Quran was revealed to the Arabs, and it was their responsibility to explain it to others.
  • Justice encompasses both rights: The Day of Judgment establishes justice for both huquq Allah (rights of Allah) and huquq al-ibad (rights of people).

docs.title: Du'a Request
docs.text: • Du'a for the father of a student (Abdullah) who had spinal disc surgery and developed a blood clot, leaving him unable to walk for four weeks. May Allah grant him shifa and a speedy recovery.

docs.title: TFS 102 - Study Notes
docs.text: Date: 2024-02-08 Surah Covered: Surah Al-Qari'ah (Chapter 101)

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covers the complete tafseer of Surah Al-Qari'ah, which describes the events of the Day of Judgment -- the striking calamity, the state of people and mountains on that day, and the weighing of deeds that determines one's eternal fate.

docs.title: Key Themes and Topics
docs.text:
• The Day of Judgment as a sudden, terrifying, and inescapable event
  • The imagery of cosmic destruction: people scattered like moths, mountains like wool
  • The connection between Surah Al-Qari'ah, Surah Al-Zilzal, and Surah Al-Adiyat
  • The weighing of deeds on the Day of Judgment (Mizan)
  • The two possible outcomes: pleasant life (Jannah) or the Pit (Hawiyah)
  • The concept that death itself initiates one's personal Qiyamah

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning Al-Qari'ah The Striking Calamity (a name for the Day of Judgment) Qara'ah To knock (especially a door at night, very hard) Al-Farash Moths Al-Mabthuth Scattered Al-Jibal Mountains (plural of Jabal) Al-'Ihn Wool Al-Manfush Fluffed up Mawazin Scales (for weighing deeds) Thaqulat Heavy (referring to the scales) Khaffat Light (referring to the scales) 'Ishah Radiyah A pleasant life Hawiyah A pit/valley of Hellfire Narun Hamiyah An intensely hot fire Umm Mother; also means abode/destination Mawsulah Relative pronoun (grammatical term, "the one who") Sighatul Madi Past tense form Sighatul Mudari' Present/future tense form Mahshar The gathering place on the Day of Judgment Kalim Allah The one who spoke to Allah (title of Musa AS) Ruqyah Shar'iyyah Permissible spiritual healing through Quran/Hadith recitation

docs.title: The Meaning of "Al-Qari'ah" (Ayat 1-3)
docs.text:
• Al-Qari'ah is an adjective (sifah); the described noun is hidden. The full meaning is "Al-Musibatul Qari'ah" (the striking calamity) or "As-Sa'atul Qari'ah" (the striking hour).
  • Allah repeats the word Al-Qari'ah three times in the opening ayat to draw attention to the extreme seriousness of this event.
  • The phrase "wa ma adraaka" (and what will make you know) was previously explained in Surah Al-Qadr. When Allah uses this phrase in the past tense (adraaka), He follows it with an answer. When He uses the present tense (yudreeka), He does not answer -- the reader must reflect on the answer himself.

docs.title: The Four Implications of "Qari'ah" -- Why This Word Was Chosen
docs.text:
The word Qari'ah (derived from knocking a door hard at night) captures four things that happen simultaneously:

  1. Unexpected arrival -- The visitor was not anticipated; similarly, the Day of Judgment will come when people are not expecting it.
  2. Disturbance -- Just as a loud knock in the middle of the night disrupts you, Qiyamah will disrupt everyone's daily routine (hadith: people will be in the middle of milking cows, painting, breastfeeding, etc.).
  3. Fear -- The knock causes fright; Qiyamah will bring an unprecedented level of terror.
  4. Disoriented movement -- You run around trying to see who is at the door; people will run around aimlessly trying to save their lives, but no one will be saved.

docs.title: Connection to Previous Surahs
docs.text:
• Connection to Surah Al-Zilzal: Both surahs describe events of the Day of Judgment.
  • Connection to Surah Al-Adiyat: The end of Al-Adiyat says secrets concealed in hearts will be exposed. This naturally raises the question: what will that day be like? Surah Al-Qari'ah answers this.

docs.title: People Like Scattered Moths (Ayah 4)
docs.text:
• "Kal-farashi al-mabthuth" -- People will be like scattered moths.
  • The resemblance is precise: moths near a fire fly in all directions with no order, no coordination, and they fall into the fire, which kills them.
  • Similarly, people will be scattered with no order, unable to plan or coordinate, running in all directions, and ultimately perishing.
  • This is describing the moment of destruction on earth, not the Hellfire (which comes later).

docs.title: Grammar Note on "Yawma" (Ayah 4)
docs.text:
• When yawma is mudaf (annexed) to a full sentence, there are two grammatical options:
  • Mabni 'ala al-fath (built on fathah) -- yawma
  • Given a haraka based on the governing agent ('amil) -- yawmu
  • Both readings are valid according to the rules covered in Tasheel al-Nahw under Zuruf.

docs.title: Mountains Like Fluffed Wool (Ayah 5)
docs.text:
• "Kal-'ihni al-manfush" -- Mountains will become like fluffed-up wool.
  • Allah mentions mountains because they are the largest things on Earth. If even mountains cannot survive, nothing can.
  • The mountains will be reduced to dust particles scattered in space, just like asteroids -- remnants of destroyed celestial bodies.
  • This event happens after all people have already died; no one will be alive to witness it.

docs.title: The Weighing of Deeds (Ayat 6-11)
docs.text:
• Heavy scales (good deeds outweigh bad): The person will have a pleasant life ('ishah radiyah).
  • Light scales (good deeds are light): The person's abode (ummuhu) will be Hawiyah -- a valley/pit of Hellfire.
  • Hawiyah is further described: "wa ma adraaka ma hiyah? Narun hamiyah" -- an intensely hot fire.
  • The word "umm" here means abode/destination (not "mother"), though both meanings exist in Arabic.
  • The grammatical note: "ma hiyah" -- originally "ma hiya" (hiya is a detached pronoun), but "ha" is added at the end for rhyme, which is common in Arabic.

docs.title: Spiritual Gravity
docs.text:
• On a day when physical gravitational pull ceases (leading to cosmic destruction), the only "gravity" that matters is the weight of one's deeds -- a spiritual gravity.
  • Good deeds create weight on the scale; this is the ruhani (spiritual) gravity.

docs.title: Lessons and Practical Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Death is your personal Qiyamah. The Prophet (SAW) said: "One who dies, his Qiyamah has already been established." No one should assume the Day of Judgment is far off -- it arrives for each person at death.

  2. Signs of the Day of Judgment have already appeared. Examples mentioned: people who were once shepherds now compete in building high-rises. Yet most people do not change their behavior despite seeing these signs fulfilled.

  3. Prepare your scales now. The only thing that will matter on that day is the weight of one's good deeds versus bad deeds. There will be no second chance to add to one's record after death.

  4. The Quran's precision in word choice. A single word (Al-Qari'ah) encapsulates an entire scenario -- unexpected arrival, disturbance, fear, and disoriented flight. This demonstrates the miraculous nature of Quranic language.

  5. No escape from Allah's decree. People will try to save themselves, but nothing -- not houses, caves, or bunkers -- will provide shelter from the destruction.

  6. The destruction is universal. It will not be limited to Earth; the heavens and the earth will all be folded up (reference to the ayah: "yawma natwi as-samawati wal-ard").

docs.title: The Location of Mahshar
docs.text: • The location of the gathering place (Mahshar) is unknown and is from the knowledge of the unseen ('ilm al-ghayb). Allah has not disclosed this, and knowing the "address" of Mahshar serves no practical purpose.

docs.title: Isra and Mi'raj -- Unique Blessing of the Prophet (SAW)
docs.text:
• No prophet was granted the closeness to Allah that the Prophet Muhammad (SAW) received during Mi'raj.
  • Adam (AS) was in Jannah, but Jannah is not the same as being near the Arsh.
  • Musa (AS) was Kalim Allah (spoke to Allah), but he spoke to Allah on Earth, not on Mi'raj.
  • This blessing is exclusive (khas) to the Prophet Muhammad (SAW).

docs.title: Adam (AS) as a Prophet
docs.text:
• A hadith explicitly states that Adam (AS) was the first prophet. A Sahabi asked the Prophet (SAW) and was told: "Yes, he was the first prophet."
  • Further proof: In the hadith of intercession on the Day of Judgment, people first approach Adam (AS) to intercede -- they would not approach him if he were not a prophet.

docs.title: Dinosaurs and Islam
docs.text:
• The existence of dinosaurs does not contradict any ayah of the Quran or hadith of the Prophet (SAW).
  • Time before Adam (AS) is irrelevant to the discussion because Allah is beyond time; there is no "before and after" for Allah.
  • What is rejected: evolutionary claims about human origins (homo sapiens evolving from earlier species) -- this contradicts Islamic texts about Adam's creation.
  • Dinosaurs themselves? No conflict with Islamic sources.

docs.title: Jinn Possession
docs.text: • Confirmed: Jinn can possess humans.

docs.title: Ruqyah (Spiritual Healing)
docs.text:
• Blowing (nafth) after reciting Quranic ayat or prescribed du'as is established in the Quran (Surah Al-Falaq) and in the Hadith.
  • Placing one's hand on the head during recitation: no specific evidence (daleel) was found for this practice.

docs.title: Jannah and Jahannam During Qiyamah
docs.text:
• There is scholarly discussion on whether Jannah and Jahannam will be temporarily "given death" during the destruction.
  • Some scholars say they will be destroyed temporarily; others say they will not, since they are not located on Earth.

docs.title: Prayer Direction in Space
docs.text:
• An astronaut in space should pray facing the Earth.
  • If humans were on Mars, they would face Earth, and the qiblah direction would change as Earth moves.

docs.title: Surah Al-Qari'ah -- Ayah-by-Ayah Summary
docs.text: Ayah Arabic (Transliteration) Summary 1 Al-Qari'ah The Striking Calamity 2 Mal-Qari'ah What is the Striking Calamity? 3 Wa ma adraaka mal-Qari'ah And what will make you know what it is? 4 Yawma yakunun-nasu kal-farashi al-mabthuth The day people will be like scattered moths 5 Wa takunul-jibalu kal-'ihni al-manfush And mountains will be like fluffed-up wool 6-7 Fa amma man thaqulat mawazinuhu, fa huwa fi 'ishatin radiyah He whose scales are heavy will have a pleasant life 8-9 Wa amma man khaffat mawazinuhu, fa ummuhu hawiyah He whose scales are light, his abode is the Pit 10-11 Wa ma adraaka ma hiyah? Narun hamiyah And what will make you know what it is? An intensely hot fire

docs.title: TFS 102 - Study Notes
docs.text: Date: 2024-02-15 Surah Covered: Surah At-Takathur (Chapter 102)

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session covers the complete tafseer of Surah At-Takathur, the 102nd chapter of the Quran. The surah addresses humanity's obsession with worldly competition and accumulation, warning of the inevitable encounter with the Hereafter. The session also explains its connection to the preceding Surah Al-Qari'ah and includes a Q&A segment.

docs.title: Key Themes and Topics
docs.text:
• Distraction through worldly competition: The core theme -- humanity is diverted from the Akhirah by the race to accumulate wealth, status, fame, and popularity.
  • Certainty of death and the Hereafter: Everyone will die and face what comes after, regardless of how distracted they are now.
  • Three levels of certainty (Yaqeen): A major conceptual framework explained through the surah -- Ilm al-Yaqeen, Ayn al-Yaqeen, and Haqq al-Yaqeen.
  • Everyone will see the Hellfire: Not necessarily enter it, but every soul will witness it.
  • Accountability for blessings (Naeem): Every blessing, even the simplest ones, will be questioned on the Day of Judgment.
  • Connection to Surah Al-Qari'ah: The sequence from destruction of the universe (Al-Qari'ah) to resurrection and reckoning (At-Takathur).
  • Regret on the Day of Judgment: Both the righteous and the wicked will experience regret, though for different reasons.

docs.title: Important Vocabulary
docs.text: Arabic Term Meaning / Explanation Alhakum "Diverted you / distracted you" -- fi'il madhi (past tense verb); "kum" is the attached pronoun (maf'ool, second person plural) Takathur Competition to increase; from the root "kathra" (abundance). When two people compete to outdo one another in accumulating something Hatta "Until" Zurtum "You visited" -- fi'il madhi from zara/yazuru Al-Maqabir "The graves" -- jamu' muntaha al-jumu' (a plural of plurals); takes tanween because of the definite article (alif-lam), so it is not ghair munsarif Kalla Aversion particle (harfu rad'in) -- translated as "Nay!" Very commonly used in the Quran Saufa Particle indicating the future; used for distant or uncertain future (as opposed to "sa-" for near future) Ta'lamoon "You will know" -- fi'il mudari', second person plural Ilm al-Yaqeen "Knowledge of certainty" -- certainty gained through reliable information Ayn al-Yaqeen "Eye of certainty" -- certainty gained through direct observation Haqq al-Yaqeen "Reality/truth of certainty" -- certainty gained through direct experience (mentioned in Surah Al-Waqi'ah) Al-Jaheem "The Hellfire" Latarawunna "Surely you will see" -- with lam (emphasis) and noon (emphasis) attached to the verb Naeem "Blessings / bounties" Alam al-Barzakh The transitional realm between death and the Day of Judgment Maf'ool Grammatical object (nasb case) Fa'il Grammatical subject (raf' case) Mudaf / Mudaf ilayh Possessive construction (e.g., Ilm al-Yaqeen: Ilm is mudaf, al-Yaqeen is mudaf ilayh)

docs.title: Ayah 1: "Alhakum at-Takathur"
docs.text:
• The competition to increase in worldly things has distracted you from the Akhirah.
  • Takathur encompasses anything that gives name, fame, or status: wealth, celebrity, popularity, social media followers, likes, views, subscribers.
  • Modern examples given: people obsessing over TikTok/Instagram likes and shares, treating 10,000 likes as if it were a Nobel Prize.
  • Also applies to competition for financial status -- example of a man working 16 hours daily at a hotel with no days off, barely seeing his children, purely to make money.
  • The wealthiest people (e.g., owners of major companies) are engaged in takathur -- competing to be number one on rich lists.

docs.title: Ayah 2: "Hatta zurtum al-maqabir"
docs.text:
• "Until you visit the graves" -- meaning this distraction continues until death.
  • The distraction only ends when you are buried.

docs.title: Ayahs 3-4: "Kalla saufa ta'lamoon, thumma kalla saufa ta'lamoon"
docs.text:
• Why "saufa" instead of "sa-"? Saufa normally indicates the distant future, while sa- indicates the near future. Here, saufa is used because of uncertainty in timing -- death could come tomorrow or in 60-70 years. The timing is uncertain, not necessarily distant.
  • Repetition of "kalla saufa ta'lamoon" is for emphasis -- what Allah wants us to know is extremely important.

docs.title: Ayah 5: "Kalla law ta'lamoon ilm al-yaqeen"
docs.text:
• "If you knew with the knowledge of certainty" -- Ilm al-Yaqeen is maf'ool (in nasb case).
  • Ilm al-Yaqeen: certainty through information from a trustworthy source. Since Allah Himself and Rasulullah (SAW) are the ones informing us about Jannah and Jahannam, and they never lie, this information constitutes knowledge of certainty.

docs.title: Ayah 6: "Latarawunna al-jaheem"
docs.text:
• "You will surely see the Hellfire."
  • Important distinction: Allah says you will SEE it, not that you will GO to it.
